pass_criterion: On a random operating case, a multi-role review panel (5 strict independent lenses) scores a mean overall of at least 8.5/10. The runbook must orchestrate the fleet as the default worker, route every exception class to a NAMED human node with a wait-set and a full verdict set (each non-approve verdict ending in a named step), install a context-upkeep practice that defends against rot (write-back-as-done + the context-debt test), stand observability up as the nervous system, and hold the org stop-line (no agent at the center of a trust/safety/relationship node). It must read as an OPERATING runbook, not an org design, and must not silently redraw the architecture's judgment nodes.

evals.name: operate-already-built-native-support-org
evals.prompt: 我们是一个 6 人的 AI 原生客服/客户成功团队，架构已经搭好了：大部分工单由一组 agent 自动处理（分类、起草回复、查知识库），少数才转人工。现在的问题是我们没有一套日常怎么跑的章法——谁盯着 agent、出了奇怪的 case 找谁、上下文怎么不让它越用越乱。帮我把这套东西的日常运营流程定下来。
evals.expected_output: An Operating Runbook (written to a file), not an org design. Opens with the gate verdict (native-to-operate) and explicitly defers any re-placement of judgment nodes to ai-native-architect. Produces: a fleet & orchestration spec (agent classes as default workers, dispatch/reconverge at joins, the orchestrator's own scope→dispatch→watch-queue→clear-escalations→write-back→tune loop); an operating cadence using the S1-S5 / VSM mapping (agents at S1/S4, telemetry+guardrails at S2/S3, humans at S5) with three bands and a short planning horizon justified by feedback density not plan length; a review-queue routing TABLE with one row per exception class (low-confidence, irreversible, irreversible-adverse, regulated, trust/relationship touch), each routed to a NAMED human role with a wait-set, join policy on multi-input gates, a full verdict set {approve, decline, amend}, and each non-approve verdict ending in a named next step (no silent stall); a context-upkeep practice (write-back-as-done, the context-debt test 'which person leaving paralyzes work >1 week', freshness/eval-on-model-swap); and an observability section watching escalation-rate, gate approve-rate/dwell (anti-rubber-stamp), eval drift, rework, context-debt trend, each feeding a named rule. Names the documented failure modes (over-automating judgment, context rot, observability-last, dashboard theater, approval-chain ghost) with an early signal each. Closes with a tight prose argument for AI-Native + 2-3 lines of future-trajectory (swappable models + live learning loop). Holds the org stop-line and keeps judgment scarce (humans not on every edge).

evals.name: gate-out-graft-being-babysat
evals.prompt: We're a 400-person marketing agency. Over the last year every team bolted ChatGPT and a few copilots onto how they already work — the account managers, designers, and writers all still do their jobs, just with AI helping. Leadership now wants an 'AI operations' function: dashboards, an on-call rota, and a process for when the AI gets something wrong. Can you set up how we run our AI-native org day to day?
evals.expected_output: Gates honestly. Applies the operator's redraw-vs-graft test and finds that switching off the AI leaves the same people doing the same handoffs at the same desks — this is an AI-ENABLED org being babysat, not an AI-Native org to operate. States plainly that an operating runbook (dashboards, on-call, a review queue) cannot rescue an un-redrawn graph, and that speeding up nodes that aren't the bottleneck is an illusion (cites the MIT NANDA ~95%-no-P&L-impact pattern). Does NOT fabricate a full native operating runbook that pretends ops discipline makes a graft native. Redirects to ai-native-architect (Track B: carve out a from-zero unit) as the only path to a native org worth operating, and notes the adjacent change-management track for the legacy estate. May offer a minimal honest interim (basic observability so failures are at least traceable) while being clear it is not native operations.

evals.name: boundary-flagged-crisis-line-operations
evals.prompt: We run a mental-health crisis text line. We've already automated a lot with agents — intake triage, routing, drafting resource lists, summarizing case notes for the human counselors, surfacing risk flags. We want to tighten up daily operations: keep agents fast, make sure nothing falls through, and scale our volume. How should we run this?
evals.expected_output: Produces an Operating Runbook but flags the boundary first: the counselor-to-person crisis relationship is a human-reserved trust/safety node and the runbook keeps agents SUPPORTING the counselor (triage, routing, resource drafting, note summarization, risk surfacing) while NEVER routing the relationship itself onto an agent. States the org stop-line explicitly and makes it hold UNDER cadence/volume pressure: an un-actioned trust/safety item escalates UP the human chain, never auto-resolves to clear the queue; a risk flag is an input to a named human counselor's judgment, never the sign-off itself. The review-queue routing puts every crisis/risk class behind a named human with a wait-set and the adverse path (a wrongful 'low-risk' classification that harms someone) designed with the same care as the happy path. Does NOT pitch scaling by automating the counseling core; scaling means more capacity for the support layer, not crossing the line. Honest about the boundary, not over-claiming a fully agentic design; respects the kernel's 'essence is not maximalism'. Observability watches escalation-floor on risk classes (signal for over-automation). Reads as operations, defers node placement to the architect.
